title: Optimizing React Function Component Re-renders Due to Changing Object References

statement.markdown: In this task, you will be working with a React function component that contains a memoized state. However, the component is re-rendering unnecessarily due to changing object references. Your goal is to optimize the component so that it does not re-render when the object reference remains the same.
statement.inputFormat: The function will receive an object with an array of tasks and a function to update the tasks.
statement.outputFormat: The function should return an object with the updated tasks and a boolean indicating whether the component has re-rendered.
statement.notes: You should assume that the input object will contain at least one task.

examples.explanation: The component has not re-rendered because the new task's reference is the same as the original task's reference.
The component has re-rendered because the new task's reference is different from the original task's reference.

hints.text: Check the `updateTasks` function to see if it's creating a new reference for the task.
Use the `===` operator to check for reference equality.

editorial.title: Editorial: Optimizing React Function Component Re-renders
editorial.intuition: To optimize the component, we need to check if the `updateTasks` function is creating a new reference for the task. If it is, we should use the `===` operator to check for reference equality.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible solution is to use the `JSON.stringify` function to convert the task object to a string and then compare the strings.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution is to use the `Object.is` function to compare the task objects directly.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use the `===` operator to compare the task objects directly and check for reference equality.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness is that the component will only re-render when the object reference changes.
editorial.dryRun: Here's an example of how to use the `===` operator to check for reference equality: `const task = {id: 1, name: 'Task 1'}; const newTask = {id: 1, name: 'Task 1'}; console.log(task === newTask); // false`
